content.text: A single attention head produces one weighted blend of value vectors per position. But natural language has many types of relationships between words simultaneously: grammatical agreement, coreference, semantic similarity, positional proximity. A single head cannot specialise in all of them at once. Multi-head attention runs
h independent attention heads in parallel
, each free to learn a different attention pattern, then merges their outputs.
Each head i has its own learned projection matrices W_Qⁱ, W_Kⁱ, W_Vⁱ. The head projects the input into a lower-dimensional subspace and runs standard scaled dot-product attention:
The h outputs are concatenated and projected back to d_model via an output weight W_O:

attrs.alt: Multi-head attention architecture showing h parallel heads each with separate Q/K/V projections, concatenation, and output projection W_O

content.text: To keep total compute similar to a single large head, each head is given a reduced dimension:

For BERT-base: d_model = 768, h = 12, so d_k = 64. The weight matrices per head are W_Qⁱ ∈ ℝ^(d_model × d_k), W_Kⁱ ∈ ℝ^(d_model × d_k), W_Vⁱ ∈ ℝ^(d_model × d_v). Together with W_O ∈ ℝ^(h·d_v × d_model), the total parameter count is:

attrs.latex: 3h \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot d_k \;+\; h \cdot d_v \cdot d_{\text{model}} = 4 \cdot d_{\text{model}}^2

content.text: This is the same as a single attention head with d_k = d_model — the split into heads adds representational diversity without increasing parameter count.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0, "d_model must be divisible by num_heads"
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads

        # One linear layer per projection, but we pack all heads together
        # W_Q, W_K, W_V each: (d_model → d_model) covering all heads at once
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_O = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

    def split_heads(self, x):
        """(batch, seq, d_model) → (batch, heads, seq, d_k)"""
        batch, seq, d_model = x.shape
        x = x.view(batch, seq, self.h, self.d_k)
        return x.transpose(1, 2)  # (batch, heads, seq, d_k)

    def forward(self, Q, K, V, mask=None):
        """
        Q, K, V: (batch, seq, d_model)
        mask:    optional (batch, 1, seq, seq) boolean or additive mask
        """
        # Project and split into heads
        Q = self.split_heads(self.W_Q(Q))  # (batch, h, seq, d_k)
        K = self.split_heads(self.W_K(K))
        V = self.split_heads(self.W_V(V))

        # Scaled dot-product attention per head
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores + mask  # additive mask; -inf for blocked positions
        attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)  # (batch, h, seq, seq)

        # Weighted sum of values
        context = torch.matmul(attn_weights, V)   # (batch, h, seq, d_k)

        # Concatenate heads and project
        batch, h, seq, d_k = context.shape
        context = context.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch, seq, h * d_k)
        return self.W_O(context), attn_weights  # (batch, seq, d_model)

# Demo
batch, seq, d_model, num_heads = 2, 10, 512, 8
mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model, num_heads)
x = torch.randn(batch, seq, d_model)
out, attn = mha(x, x, x)   # self-attention: Q = K = V = x
print(out.shape)    # (2, 10, 512)
print(attn.shape)   # (2, 8, 10, 10) — one matrix per head

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim, num_heads, batch_first=True)
mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=512, num_heads=8, batch_first=True)

batch, seq, d_model = 2, 10, 512
x = torch.randn(batch, seq, d_model)

# Self-attention: query = key = value = x
# need_weights=False skips materialising the attention matrix (faster)
out, attn_weights = mha(x, x, x, need_weights=True)
print(out.shape)          # (2, 10, 512)
print(attn_weights.shape) # (2, 10, 10) — averaged across heads by default

# For decoder self-attention (causal mask)
causal_mask = nn.Transformer.generate_square_subsequent_mask(seq)
out_causal, _ = mha(x, x, x, attn_mask=causal_mask)
print(out_causal.shape)   # (2, 10, 512)
Heads specialise through gradient descent, not by design. Research (Voita et al. 2019, Clark et al. 2019) consistently finds distinct patterns:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased', output_attentions=True)

text = "She saw the man with the telescope"
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt')

with torch.no_grad():
    out = model(**inputs)

# out.attentions: tuple of (batch, num_heads, seq, seq), one per layer
layer5 = out.attentions[5][0]   # (12 heads, seq, seq)
tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs['input_ids'][0])

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 4, figsize=(14, 9))
for i, ax in enumerate(axes.flat):
    ax.imshow(layer5[i].numpy(), cmap='Blues', vmin=0, vmax=1)
    ax.set_title(f'Head {i+1}', fontsize=8)
    ax.set_xticks(range(len(tokens))); ax.set_xticklabels(tokens, rotation=45, fontsize=6)
    ax.set_yticks(range(len(tokens))); ax.set_yticklabels(tokens, fontsize=6)
plt.suptitle('Layer 5 — 12 heads, BERT-base')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
After concatenation, each head's output occupies a separate block of the h·d_v-dimensional vector. W_O is a learned linear map that

and projects back to d_model. Without W_O, the heads would be independent and the output space would be partitioned rather than fused. W_O lets the model learn how to weight and combine each head's perspective into a single unified representation.

Multi-head attention is cheaper than it sounds. Because each head uses d_k = d_model/h, the total FLOPs are identical to a single head at d_model. The cost is the same; the benefit is representational diversity across h subspaces.

A transformer's central challenge is representing every word's meaning in context simultaneously. The word 'bank' means something different in 'river bank' versus 'savings bank' versus 'blood bank'. These distinctions live in different subspaces of the embedding: semantic content, syntactic role, topic domain. A single attention head computes a single weighted mixture of value vectors — it can attend to multiple positions, but with one set of query/key/value projections it is forced to learn a single type of relevance.

Multiple heads allow the model to represent several types of relevance simultaneously. Head 1 might ask which words are syntactically related to this one, while Head 2 asks which words are semantically similar and Head 3 asks what appeared nearby in the original text. All three answers are assembled into a single rich representation by the output projection W_O. This is why removing even a few heads from a trained BERT model degrades performance noticeably: each head captures a distinct slice of the attention signal.
Representational diversity at no extra cost
: d_k = d_model/h, so h parallel heads use the same total parameters as one big head. The split gives diversity for free.
: A single head must spread its attention across all relevant positions. Multiple heads can each attend sharply to a different aspect of the context.
: Heads discover their roles via gradient descent. No hard-coded rules are needed — the model learns which head should handle syntax and which should handle coreference.
: Pruning experiments (Michel et al. 2019) show many heads are redundant in trained models, suggesting multiple heads also provide a form of ensemble regularisation.
: All h heads are computed simultaneously via batched matrix multiplications on GPU — no sequential dependency between heads.

The attention matrix has O(n^2) memory cost per head, and with h heads the total attention state is O(h * n^2 * d_k). For GPT-3 with 96 heads and a 2048-token context this is gigabytes of intermediate state. FlashAttention (Dao et al. 2022) addresses this by computing attention in tiles without materialising the full n-by-n matrix, reducing memory from O(n^2) to O(n) while maintaining exact numerical results.

Not all heads contribute equally. Voita et al. (2019) showed that in a 6-layer, 8-head encoder, large fractions of heads could be pruned with minimal BLEU score drop on translation. The number of heads h should be chosen based on available compute and d_model rather than information-theoretic grounds. Keeping d_k at least 32 prevents attention distributions from becoming too sharp (near one-hot), which would reduce expressive capacity.
When to Use (and When Not to Use)

Use multi-head attention when your task requires relating arbitrary positions in a sequence: machine translation, question answering, document summarisation, and code generation. For tasks where locality matters more than global context — short time series, small convolutional feature maps — single-head attention or purely convolutional blocks may be more compute-efficient.

The number of heads h must divide d_model evenly. Standard configurations: h = 8 for d_model = 512 (original Transformer), h = 12 for d_model = 768 (BERT-base), h = 16 for d_model = 1024 (BERT-large), h = 96 for d_model = 12288 (GPT-3). Avoid configurations with d_k < 32 since very small head dimensions make softmax distributions too sharp.

The most frequent implementation error is omitting the 1/sqrt(d_k) scaling before softmax. Without it, dot products between d_k-dimensional vectors grow as O(d_k), pushing softmax into saturation where gradients vanish. This was the reason early attention model training was unstable.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

# Assume: Q, K, V have shape (batch, heads, seq, d_k)
batch, h, seq, d_k = 2, 8, 64, 64
Q = torch.randn(batch, h, seq, d_k)
K = torch.randn(batch, h, seq, d_k)
V = torch.randn(batch, h, seq, d_k)

# Wrong: no scaling — scores grow with d_k, softmax saturates near one-hot
scores_wrong = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1))
attn_wrong   = F.softmax(scores_wrong, dim=-1)

# Correct: scale by sqrt(d_k) before softmax
scores_right = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(d_k)
attn_right   = F.softmax(scores_right, dim=-1)

# Wrong: padding mask set to 0 AFTER softmax (still attends to padding)
mask = torch.zeros(batch, 1, 1, seq)  # 0 = attend, 1 = mask
pad_pos = 50  # tokens after position 50 are padding
mask[:, :, :, pad_pos:] = 1
scores_masked_wrong = scores_right.clone()
scores_masked_wrong[mask.bool()] = 0  # setting to 0 after softmax is too late

# Correct: set masked positions to -inf BEFORE softmax
scores_masked = scores_right.masked_fill(mask.bool(), float('-inf'))
attn_padded = F.softmax(scores_masked, dim=-1)  # padding positions get 0 weight

# Wrong: choosing h=7 when d_model=512 (not evenly divisible)
# 512 / 7 = 73.14... -> error in split_heads
# Correct: use h that divides d_model: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512

print('Correct attention shape:', attn_right.shape)  # (2, 8, 64, 64)
: It dispatches to FlashAttention automatically on CUDA with bf16/fp16 and handles masking correctly.
Apply dropout to attention weights during training
: A rate of 0.1 prevents heads from over-concentrating on single positions.
: Very small head dimensions produce overly peaked attention distributions.
: Biases in key and query projections add parameters without helping; most modern implementations omit them.
: PyTorch's fused implementation handles masks correctly and supports both batch-first and sequence-first conventions.

FLOP count for multi-head attention is O(n^2 * d + n * d^2) per layer, where n is sequence length and d is d_model. The n^2 term from the attention matrix dominates for long sequences; the d^2 term from QKV and output projections dominates for short sequences. For typical inputs (512-2048 tokens), feed-forward layers are the main bottleneck.

For long contexts (8k-128k tokens), the n^2 cost is prohibitive. Efficient variants address this: Longformer uses local + global attention windows in O(n); Linformer projects keys and values to a fixed size; FlashAttention maintains O(n^2) FLOPs but achieves O(n) memory. Modern long-context models use Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) to share key/value heads across multiple query heads, reducing KV-cache memory during autoregressive inference.
: PyTorch built-in; supports batch_first, KV caching, additive and boolean masks.
(PyTorch 2.0+): Dispatches to FlashAttention on CUDA with bf16/fp16.
: Meta's library of efficient attention kernels including memory-efficient attention and sparse patterns.
: Visualisation and mechanistic interpretability tools for inspecting per-head attention patterns.
: All models expose output_attentions=True to return per-head attention weights for research.

Attention mechanisms expose a specific adversarial vulnerability: attention hijacking. In prompt injection attacks, adversarial tokens are inserted into context to attract disproportionate attention from other tokens, steering the model toward attacker-controlled outputs. Since attention scores are determined by learned dot-product similarity, carefully crafted token embeddings can exploit high dot-product scores in specific heads.

For long-context models, the lost-in-the-middle problem (Liu et al. 2023) is a reliability concern: when relevant information appears in the middle of a long context, attention weights are empirically lower than for information at the beginning or end, causing the model to overlook it. Mitigations include re-ranking retrieved documents to place the most relevant at context boundaries and using position encodings (RoPE, ALiBi) that maintain attention quality across long distances.
Real-World Example: Building a Transformer Encoder Block

A transformer encoder block combines multi-head self-attention with a position-wise feed-forward network (FFN), each wrapped in a residual connection and layer normalisation. This is the unit that repeats 12 times in BERT-base, 24 times in BERT-large, and 96 times in GPT-3. Understanding how multi-head attention fits into this block is essential for implementing and debugging transformer models.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Efficient packed multi-head attention."""
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0
        self.h  = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        # Pack Q, K, V projections into a single matrix for efficiency
        self.qkv = nn.Linear(d_model, 3 * d_model, bias=False)
        self.out = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, mask=None) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, T, D = x.shape
        # Single matmul produces Q, K, V together
        qkv = self.qkv(x).view(B, T, 3, self.h, self.d_k)
        qkv = qkv.permute(2, 0, 3, 1, 4)   # (3, B, h, T, d_k)
        Q, K, V = qkv.unbind(0)             # each: (B, h, T, d_k)

        # PyTorch 2.0+ dispatches to FlashAttention when possible
        x_out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(
            Q, K, V,
            attn_mask=mask,
            dropout_p=self.drop.p if self.training else 0.0,
        )  # (B, h, T, d_k)

        # Merge heads
        x_out = x_out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, D)
        return self.out(x_out)


class FeedForward(nn.Module):
    """Position-wise FFN: two linear layers with a GELU activation."""
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, d_ff: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)


class TransformerEncoderBlock(nn.Module):
    """Single transformer encoder block: MHA + FFN with pre-LayerNorm."""
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, d_ff: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.attn  = MultiHeadAttention(d_model, num_heads, dropout)
        self.ff    = FeedForward(d_model, d_ff, dropout)
        self.drop  = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, mask=None) -> torch.Tensor:
        # Pre-LayerNorm residual (used by modern LLMs; original paper used post-norm)
        x = x + self.drop(self.attn(self.norm1(x), mask))
        x = x + self.drop(self.ff(self.norm2(x)))
        return x


# Build a 6-layer BERT-like encoder
d_model, num_heads, d_ff = 512, 8, 2048   # d_ff = 4 * d_model is standard
encoder = nn.Sequential(*[
    TransformerEncoderBlock(d_model, num_heads, d_ff)
    for _ in range(6)
])

batch_size, seq_len = 4, 128
x = torch.randn(batch_size, seq_len, d_model)
out = encoder(x)
print(f'Input:  {x.shape}')   # (4, 128, 512)
print(f'Output: {out.shape}') # (4, 128, 512) — same shape, richer representations

params = sum(p.numel() for p in encoder.parameters())
print(f'Total parameters: {params:,}')  # ~18M for 6 layers
— each position can only attend to positions at or before it. This is enforced via a causal mask applied before the softmax, setting attention scores for future positions to
so they receive zero weight after softmax.
During training, the causal mask allows all positions to be computed in parallel despite the autoregressive constraint. At inference, tokens are generated one at a time, and the

stores previously computed key and value tensors so they do not need to be recomputed for each new token. Without the KV cache, inference would require O(n) forward passes each of cost O(n), giving O(n^2) total cost; with it, each new token requires O(1) attention ops against the cached KV, giving O(n) total cost.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Causal mask for autoregressive attention
def make_causal_mask(seq_len: int, device: torch.device) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Upper-triangular mask: -inf for positions j > i (future tokens)."""
    mask = torch.full((seq_len, seq_len), float('-inf'), device=device)
    mask = torch.triu(mask, diagonal=1)   # keep diagonal (self) and below
    return mask.unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)  # (1, 1, seq, seq) for broadcasting

# Demonstration with nn.MultiheadAttention
mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=512, num_heads=8, batch_first=True)

batch, seq, d = 2, 10, 512
x = torch.randn(batch, seq, d)
causal_mask = make_causal_mask(seq, x.device)  # (1, 1, 10, 10)

out, weights = mha(x, x, x, attn_mask=causal_mask.squeeze(0).squeeze(0))
# weights[b, i, j] == 0 for j > i: future positions get no attention
print('Output shape:        ', out.shape)      # (2, 10, 512)
print('Attention weights shape:', weights.shape) # (2, 10, 10)
print('Future masking works:', (weights[:, :5, 5:] == 0).all().item())  # True

# Built-in causal helper in nn.Transformer
causal = nn.Transformer.generate_square_subsequent_mask(seq, device=x.device)
out2, _ = mha(x, x, x, attn_mask=causal)
print('Using built-in mask:', out2.shape)
The original Transformer (Vaswani et al. 2017) uses an encoder-decoder architecture for sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation. The decoder applies

to attend to the encoder's output: the queries come from the decoder, while the keys and values come from the encoder. This is how the decoder "reads" the source sentence while generating each target word.

In cross-attention with multiple heads, each head can specialise in different aspects of the source. One head might focus on aligning a target noun to a source noun (syntactic alignment); another might attend to the verb of the source clause regardless of position (semantic alignment). This multi-head cross-attention is why neural machine translation models learned to produce more accurate translations than earlier attention mechanisms.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Cross-attention: decoder attends to encoder output
mha = nn.MultiheadAttention(embed_dim=512, num_heads=8, batch_first=True)

batch      = 2
src_len    = 20   # source sequence length (e.g. English sentence)
tgt_len    = 15   # target sequence length (e.g. French sentence, being generated)
d_model    = 512

encoder_out = torch.randn(batch, src_len, d_model)  # K and V from encoder
decoder_ctx = torch.randn(batch, tgt_len, d_model)  # Q from decoder

# Cross-attention: Q from decoder, K/V from encoder
out, attn_weights = mha(
    query=decoder_ctx,    # (batch, tgt_len, d_model)
    key=encoder_out,      # (batch, src_len, d_model)
    value=encoder_out,    # (batch, src_len, d_model)
)
print('Cross-attention output:', out.shape)         # (2, 15, 512)
print('Attention weights:     ', attn_weights.shape) # (2, 15, 20)
# attn_weights[b, t, s] = how much target position t attends to source position s
# This is the alignment matrix: high weight = the target word came from that source word
Multiple heads give multiple attention perspectives
in parallel, each in a lower-dimensional subspace of size d_k = d_model/h.
all heads into a unified d_model vector.
Same parameter count as one big head
: syntactic, coreference, positional, and semantic heads emerge automatically.
before softmax to prevent gradient vanishing in deep networks.
reduces attention memory to O(n) — use scaled_dot_product_attention in PyTorch 2.0+ to get it automatically.
is the modern inference-time optimisation — used by LLaMA 3, Mistral, and Gemma to reduce KV-cache memory.
in every major transformer: BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA, Claude, and Gemini.
